title: Variance of sums of arithmetic functions over primes in short intervals
speaker: Jon Keating
abstract: Goldston & Montgomery and Montgomery & Soundararajan have established formulae for the variance of sums of the von Magoldt function over short intervals (i.e. for the variance of the number of primes in these intervals) assuming, respectively, the pair-correlation conjecture and the Hardy-Littlewood conjecture. I will discuss the generalisation of these formulae to other arithmetic functions associated with the Selberg class of L-functions, in the context of both zero statistics and arithmetic correlations.

proof_stages.label: Classical short-interval variance for primes
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins with the von Mangoldt function, the prime number theorem, and the Goldston-Montgomery/Montgomery-Soundararajan conjecture for the variance of prime counts in short intervals, including its range in h and its constant term.

proof_stages.label: Classical conjectural bridges to the variance formula
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains how Hardy-Littlewood prime-pair correlations, Montgomery's pair-correlation function for Riemann zeros, and the ratios conjecture each lead to the classical variance prediction, with the zero-correlation route giving the leading behavior and the ratio route supplying finer constants.

proof_stages.label: Function-field validation of the classical picture
proof_stages.summary: As background evidence, the function-field analogue is described: monic irreducible polynomials replace primes, zeta zeros become eigenvalues in conjugacy classes, Katz equidistribution gives the analogue of Montgomery's conjecture, and the Goldston-Montgomery variance formula follows unconditionally in the q-to-infinity limit.

proof_stages.label: Primitive L-function setup for generalized coefficients
proof_stages.summary: The speaker sets up a general primitive L-function, its Euler product, logarithmic-derivative coefficients Lambda_L(n), pole order, functional equation, degree, conductor, GRH notation for zeros, and the corresponding short-interval variance to be computed.

proof_stages.label: Generalized zero-pair conjecture versus shifted-coefficient correlations
proof_stages.summary: For general L-functions the direct Hardy-Littlewood-style shifted coefficient correlation is expected to have no main term, while the generalized Montgomery/Murty-Perelli zero-pair conjecture and the L-function ratios conjecture predict a zeta-like pair-correlation with degree and conductor entering in controlled ways.

proof_stages.label: Tauberian transfer and the two-regime variance theorem
proof_stages.summary: Using the Goldston-Montgomery explicit-formula/Tauberian strategy, the refined conjecture for F_L is shown to be equivalent to a variance asymptotic with a first degree-and-conductor-dependent regime for b<1/d_L and a second universal saturation regime for b>1/d_L.

proof_stages.label: Numerical evidence for the predicted regimes
proof_stages.summary: Numerical plots of the normalized variance are presented as evidence: the zeta case follows the classical linear prediction, while degree-two examples show the predicted linear-to-saturated transition.

proof_stages.label: Function-field degree-two theorem via equidistribution
proof_stages.summary: The final part sketches joint work on degree-two L-functions over function fields, using elliptic curves over F_q(t), admissible squarefree conductors, twisting, and Katz-style equidistribution to obtain an exact matrix-integral variance formula, min(n,2 deg Q-1), matching the two-regime phenomenon unconditionally for that class.
